"Why did it take so long for the political establishment to take this seriously? Because Hollywood's business has been leeching away for the last three decades," entertainment lawyer Jonathan Handel told Fox News Digital.

StageRunner founder and CEO Jason Fisher, formerly head of production at Disney, Paramount and AMC, said he began noticing the rise of runaway production over two decades ago.

"Production has been slowly and then speeding up really over the last two years, leaving California for other states and other countries and really nothing was done until it was almost too late," he told Fox News Digital.

Several popular episodic television series were filmed outside of California, including Paramount’s "Landman" in Fort Worth, Texas and Netflix’s "Stranger Things" in Atlanta, Georgia.

Despite LA-based studios’ efforts to film locally, the substantial savings available outside of California made production in the Golden State increasingly difficult to justify.

"When I worked at the studios, we talked about a lot. ‘What could we do to keep the show here?’ And when you get to that place where you're just saving millions of dollars by going to some place other than California, they're just aren't any other levers to pull," Fisher said.

FROM ‘SCREAM’ TO STREAM: JAMIE KENNEDY REVEALS HOW HOLLYWOOD LOST ITS WAY AND WHY HE WON'T GIVE UP ON LA

The decline in production has sent ripple effects across California’s economy, with more than 80 production service businesses closing in Los Angeles since 2022 because of a lack of work and industry strikes, according to CBS News.

Experts say production work has been hemorrhaging for decades and that Los Angeles has lost tens of thousands of film and television jobs over the years.

"It is decimation, and it's not just like the film rental companies, the production companies — it trickles down to the restaurants, the people supported by the industry here," Ellison said.

Ellison’s camera rental house, Shadowcast Pictures, provided cameras, lighting and other filmmaking gear to production crews for nearly two decades before becoming one of the businesses forced to close.

"We've been injecting our own money to keep the company going. I know lots of others are doing that, and it came to the point where we finally had to say enough because we've had three years of downturn," Ellison told Fox News Digital.

Industry workers who once spent 15 to 20 days a month on set are now working as few as one to 10 days, if they are "lucky," Ellison said. He added that many are hoping to secure jobs on long-running television series in search of greater stability.

"I have networks of friends who are actors, who are crew members, who are directors, who are writers trying to get scripts sold. And if you ask one of them, ‘Have you worked lately?’ You get looked at like you're crazy," Handel said.

"Because we all know the answer, which is … in most cases, no," he added.

The production downturn comes amid major turmoil within Los Angeles' entertainment space. Television City, an iconic Los Angeles studio lot where "The Sonny and Cher Show" and "The Price Is Right" were filmed could be up for sale as its owners struggle with all-time low production levels and $357 million worth of debt.

DANNY MCBRIDE SAYS DITCHING LA WAS THE BEST MOVE FOR HIS CAREER AND FAMILY

"I don't know if in a year, two years from now, we're going to be able to call it an industry because so much has left," Jay Ellison said.

"We have Quixote Studios closing their stages here. Then we're going to have possibly this major merger between Paramount and Warner Brothers, are they going to keep two massive studio lots? A lot of questions being raised about that right now," he added.

Meanwhile, Gov. Newsom recently touted California’s Film and Television Tax Credit Program, which he said is on track to generate $6.6 billion in direct production spending back to the Golden State.

"The world we invented is now competing against us ... We've got to step up our game," Newsom said last year when discussing the incentive.

Ellison and Fisher agreed that state leaders were "too late" in recognizing the production problem plaguing the birthplace of entertainment, questioning whether Hollywood could ever fully reclaim its former dominance, even with Newsom’s expanded tax credit.

"The incentive has brought back some production. It's keeping things going," Ellison said. "It's just not nearly enough when you're competing with so many other foreign countries."

"I think it's a start, but I don't think it is enough," said Fisher. "Everyone who works in the industry understands California is the most expensive place to shoot, and it's the most challenging place to shoot. Getting those incentives is still a real challenge."

Experts said financial incentives are not the only factor luring studios elsewhere. Rival production hubs, most notably Atlanta, have developed extensive infrastructure, experienced local crews and vendor networks.

Ellison said that those investments have put Los Angeles in a position where it no longer has a competitive edge against other cities in production resources.

"Other jurisdictions around the world have built up their own capabilities, and they are now attractive places to shoot. And there's only so much that you can stretch the California budget in terms of tax incentives," Handel said.

Now that studios have access to well-established and less expensive production centers elsewhere, Handel said, California will face difficulty winning business back.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE COVERAGE OF MEDIA AND CULTURE

"All of that is now what California is fighting against, and it's a tough uphill climb," the entertainment lawyer told Fox News Digital. "It would have been easier to keep the business here than it will be to claw it back with improved tax incentives in California."

In a statement to Fox News Digital, Gavin Newsom’s office said California remains the nation’s top destination for film and television despite "unprecedented competition."

"That’s exactly why Governor Newsom took historic action to more than double California's Film & Television Tax Credit Program, strengthening California's competitive edge by keeping jobs and investment where the industry's unmatched workforce, infrastructure and creative talent have made it the epicenter of the world's entertainment industry for more than a century," the statement said.

"In its first year, the expanded program is projected to generate $6.6 billion in direct production spending across 170 projects, bringing nearly 35,000 cast and crew jobs to communities across the state," it continued.

While some blame California’s political leadership over Hollywood’s production decimation, others are also pointing fingers at the studios themselves.

"Not nearly enough has been done, but at the same time, it's also not up to our elected officials entirely to bring production back to California," Ellison said. "The studios are the ones deciding where they're going to ultimately film, and they're choosing to film overseas because it's cheaper in the end."

Fisher, who held executive roles at major studios, defended the corporations, saying they ultimately bear a "fiduciary responsibility" to shareholders.

"We have a very sharp-edged capitalist system, and the studios are doing what businesses frequently do," Handel said.

Fox News Digital asked the industry experts whether Los Angeles could reclaim its position as the world’s dominant film and television production hub.

"I think that it's not too late. I think it can get rebuilt, but action needs to happen immediately," Ellison said. "It needed to happen months ago."

item.title: Could this African nation become Trump's next terror target as jihadists close in on power?
item.description: Mali's gold and lithium deposits add a minerals dimension as Trump's team reportedly weighs strikes against al Qaeda-linked terrorists in the Sahel.
item.content:encoded:
JOHANNESBURG: The U.S. is reportedly considering airstrikes against al Qaeda-linked terrorists in Mali, according to regional analysts and recent media reports.

The military junta that has been running this West African country in the Sahel region since coups in both 2020 and 2021 turned away from France and the U.S. to get military protection from Russia and its Africa Corps mercenaries against jihadist groups.

But now the largest of these groups, Jama’at Nusrat al-Islam wal-Muslimin (JNIM), which has Mali’s capital under siege, is credited, along with another group, for killing 50 Malian soldiers this month, for the death of the country’s defense minister earlier this year and has reportedly sent battalions of Russian soldiers retreating on at least two occasions this year.

ISIS POISED TO EXPLOIT HISTORIC US IRAQ EXIT, TERROR GROUP 'ACTIVELY WAITING IN THE GAPS,' EXPERT WARNS

JNIM is said to want to change the country into an Islamist Jihadist state. If they succeed, Mali would be the first country in the world to be run by a jihadist group. The organization was designated officially as a Foreign Terrorist Organization during the first Trump administration in 2018.

Islamic State (ISIS) jihadist groups are also operating freely in Mali, and are linked to terror groups in neighboring Nigeria.

A State Department official didn’t respond to a Washington Post report that the U.S. is considering strikes against JNIM in Mali, but used strong language Thursday when talking to Fox News Digital, leading analysts to suggest the time for talking may be over.

"The United States unequivocally condemns the terrorist activity in Mali," the official said, adding, "in the Sahel, the United States is committed to promoting regional ownership and cooperation against terrorist threats, and monitoring indicators and warnings of threats to our homeland."

Edmund Fitton-Brown, a former British and United Nations diplomat, said the U.S. could order military action, to primarily try and stop jihadists becoming entrenched in Mali. Fitton-Brown, a senior fellow at the Foundation for Defense of Democracies, told Fox News Digital, "Mali is mainly important to the U.S. because, as a failed state becoming a safe haven for al Qaeda and ISIS, it looks like the first of several African dominoes likely to fall."

Retired U.S. Air Force Maj. Gen. Kenneth P. Ekman, who until last year, served as the Department of War’s West Africa Coordination Element lead, said that with strike action the administration may be able to persuade Mali’s rulers to turn away from Russia, and towards the U.S. for protection – and purchases of military equipment.

RESOURCE-RICH NATION PRAISES US TIES AMID WASHINGTON-BEIJING CRITICAL MINERALS RACE

Ekman told Fox News Digital, "JNIM has a stranglehold on the government and much of the population. The Russians are woefully ineffective and extractive. Cooperation with U.S. forces offers a disproportionate and positive impact."

Ekman said that U.S. forces have supported partner-led operations against JNIM before, as recently as 2022, adding "⁠recent proposals amount to returning to similar cooperation against a mutual threat."

The administration may also be in a minerals frame of mind. Mali is one of Africa’s largest gold-producing nations. It also has substantial deposits of lithium, used extensively in rechargeable batteries.

Fitton-Brown said that "President Trump may be less reluctant than his predecessor to work with the juntas in Mali, Burkina Faso and Niger, if they are losing faith in their Russian friends."

He continued, "The U.S. is certainly a more capable partner. France has already been displaced, so all three countries might accept U.S. help if it comes only with strings attached that don’t offend or disadvantage them. Whether even the U.S. is capable of stopping the political decline of these countries, and the rise of militant Islam, is another matter. That cannot be achieved by military force alone."

The FDD’s Fitton-Brown said U.S. assets such as aircraft may not be directly involved if there is a strike, "The U.S. might work with French and other intelligence services to develop local intelligence for strikes on JNIM leadership, but this is unlikely to achieve much. The organization is resilient and will be able to replace those killed."

A former senior Department of War official told Fox News Digital the U.S. has at its disposal cruise missiles and long-range bombers. Attack drones would probably not come into play, and the U.S. Naval Institute’s Fleet Tracker shows no American aircraft carriers anywhere near African waters, effectively ruling out carrier operations.

However, Ekman believes airstrikes are under consideration, noting "JNIM and ISIS are a threat to U.S. interests, and are ascendant in West Africa."

item.title: Purported Nancy Guthrie note includes unusual demand that breaks basic ransom rule: retired FBI negotiator
item.description: A retired FBI negotiator says the first ransom note in the Nancy Guthrie Tucson kidnapping case is inconsistent with a typical ransom motive.
item.content:encoded:
The latest revelations about the purported first ransom note in the Nancy Guthrie case appear inconsistent with a kidnapping for ransom, according to a retired FBI negotiator.

"The fact that they wanted to cut off communication quickly suggests that they may not have had kidnapping for ransom as a primary motive, even though that's what this says," said Jason Pack, a retired FBI supervisory agent. "It doesn't seem like how a usual kidnapping goes down."

He said typical kidnappers want to keep communication channels open to negotiate payment and maintain leverage over the victim's family, making the reported demand for no further contact highly unusual.

NEW DETAILS FROM NANCY GUTHRIE RANSOM DEMAND REVEALED, 'HER LIFE IS IN YOUR HANDS': REPORT

"The most telling sign is this no negotiation, no further contact line," Pack told Fox News Digital. "Genuine kidnappers wanna keep the line of communication open."

The sender did, however, incorporate known pressure tactics, including setting a deadline, making threats and telling the family that Guthrie knew what would happen if they failed to comply, Pack said.

But cutting off communication before the deadlines passed was also unusual, he added.

NANCY GUTHRIE RANSOM NOTE CLAIMED THAT SHE HAD DIED, BEEN ‘BURIED WITH NATURE,’ SOURCE SAYS

Guthrie was taken from her home in Tucson's Catalina Foothills neighborhood in the early morning hours of Feb. 1, according to the Pima County Sheriff's Department and the FBI. She has not been seen since, and no proof of life was ever provided.

In a second purported ransom note, the sender indicated Guthrie had died soon after she was taken and been buried "with nature."

New excerpts from what is reportedly the first note received by local media shed light on why Pack believes the communication departed from a traditional ransom demand.

NANCY GUTHRIE RANSOM NOTES DON'T MATCH SUSPECT'S BEHAVIOR, PROFILER SAYS: 'I DON'T BELIEVE THEY'RE REAL'

Briana Whitney, who was working for the Phoenix-based AZ Family news outlet when the case broke, joined "Crime Junkie" host Ashley Flowers on Wednesday to discuss the note her former outlet received. "Crime Junkie" declined to share copies of the text.

"Hello Savannah, we have your mother Nancy," she began, reading from what she said was the first note sent to local media. "She is safe but scared. She will be held for ransom, and once payment is received, she will be released unharmed. We will be holding her for a maximum of seven days."

As previously conveyed by authorities, the sender gave two deadlines with increased money demands the longer it took.

"Once payment is received to the Bitcoin address below, she will be released within 12 hours of deposit to a safe drop-off location back in Tucson," Whitney read. "If the payment is not received by the last deadline on Monday the 9th at 5 p.m., she will be killed. Your mother is aware of this, and her life is in your hands."

FOLLOW THE FOX TRUE CRIME TEAM ON X

The sender left no room for further dialogue.

"It is in the best interest of everyone to have this completed as soon as possible," the note reportedly continued. "You will not be able to contact me from here on out. There will be no negotiation. Do not play games. Law enforcement will not be able to help you."

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST TRUE CRIME NEWS

The note also included details that may have only been available to investigators and the suspect at the time, including the location of Guthrie's Apple Watch and details about the smashed floodlight in her backyard — but even that is not enough to pin down kidnapping for ransom as a motive, Pack said.

"The details about the security camera and the spotlight that was out, that proves that they know what the home was like, what the environment may have been like, but it's not a real proof of life," he said.

SEND US A TIP

In most ransom kidnappings, he said, the abductors will want to prove they have possession of the victim and that the victim is alive.

"All these things put together don't seem really like they go with the kidnapping for ransom," he said.

Investigators appear to be split on the notes' authenticity. While one person has already pleaded guilty to sending an impostor ransom note to Guthrie's other daughter, Annie, and her husband Tommaso Cioni, that case is not believed to be connected to the notes sent to local media stations.

"The FBI and its task force partners have received several ransom notes over the course of this investigation," the FBI's Phoenix office said in a statement in June. "Some have been deemed to be extortion attempts without legitimacy. Other ransom demands may potentially be legitimate and are still being investigated as such. This case continues to be investigated as a kidnapping for ransom case. The FBI has and will continue to offer all assistance possible in the investigation — however local authorities remain the lead."

LISTEN TO THE NEW 'CRIME & JUSTICE WITH DONNA ROTUNNO' PODCAST

Some investigators said they were confident that the ransom demands are all bogus, while others are cautious about reaching that conclusion while Guthrie's whereabouts remain unknown, federal law enforcement sources told Fox News and Fox News Digital. Still, the two notes that Whitney mentioned have been deemed potentially authentic by investigators and by the Guthrie family.

The only known suspect remains publicly unidentified — a masked intruder who appeared on Nest doorbell camera video that the FBI recovered with help from Google.

He is described as standing between 5 feet, 9 inches and 5 feet, 10 inches tall with an average build. He was wearing a black Ozark Trail backpack. It remains unclear whether he acted alone.

The sum of rewards in the case is over $1.2 million.

Anyone with information is asked to dial 1-800-CALL-FBI. Tips can also be submitted anonymously to Tucson's Crime Stoppers affiliate at 520-88-CRIME.

item.title: Mamdani warning to wealthy New Yorkers about 'fair share' sparks online firestorm: 'Full blown communist'
item.description: Zohran Mamdani's viral post celebrating New York City's new pied-à-terre tax on $5M+ second homes drew sharp backlash from conservative critics.
item.content:encoded:
New York City socialist mayor Zohran Mamdani is facing heat on social media after his post celebrating the state’s new pied-à-terre tax on New Yorkers with a second home.

"If you have a second home in New York City worth more than $5 million, check your mailbox when you’re back in the five boroughs — because you've got mail," Mamdani posted on X, touting the tax on specific second properties introduced by New York Gov. Kathy Hochul that he supported going into effect soon.

"Today, we sent notification letters to property owners, letting them know that our new pied-à-terre tax is coming soon," Mamdani continued. "The best city in the world deserves the best parks, libraries, and schools in the world. That's only possible when we all pay our fair share. "

As of Friday morning, Mamdani’s post had been viewed over 10 million times and conservatives on social media blasted the mayor and warned that the exodus of wealthy New Yorkers will only continue under this new plan.

HOCHUL CONFRONTED ON STUDY SHOWING MILLIONAIRE EXODUS IN NEW YORK CITY

"Never elect a politician who: (1) sees your money as his slush fund, (2) loves Karl Marx, or (3) uses French words like ‘pied-à-terre’ to disguise new, confiscatory taxes he wants to impose to redistribute wealth," Republican Sen. Mike Lee (R-UT) posted on X.

"Socialism - Florida’s best real estate agent," Republican Sen. Ashley Moody (R-FL) posted on X.

"NYC’s pied-à-terre tax hasn’t even started and brokers already say owners are calling to sell," GOP strategist and attorney Mehek Cooke posted on X. "Empty units don’t tip [doormen] or pay supers. You’re not taxing the rich. You’re firing the working class that depended on them. "

"More incitement," Judicial Watch President Tom Fitton posted on X. "Communist @NYCMayor celebrates a confiscatory tax targeted at a select few. "

MAMDANI SETS INTERNET ABLAZE WITH VIDEO EXPLAINING CAVE ON PLAN TO ARREST NETANYAHU: 'WHAT A JOKE'

"What will the excuse be when literally nothing improves after all these tax increases?" New York City Republican Councilwoman Vickie Paladino posted on X. "Will they just demand even more, or will they tell us to ignore our own eyes as they insist they’ve actually fixed everything? Both? "

"The condescension towards the people who already pay a ton of property taxes to NYC," Washington Free Beacon investigative reporter Chuck Ross posted on X. "He thinks you're an immoral person and worthy of mockery if you have a second home. ‘when you're back in the five boroughs.’"

"That's only possible when we all pay our fair share,’ says the man who’s literally lived off of taxpayer dollars for years, produces nothing, and is concentrating the burdens on others—others who’ve contributed exponentially more to the city in both taxes and charitable contributions than he could possibly hope to contribute in 10 lifetimes, I might add," Manhattan Institute fellow Rafael Mangual posted on X.

"You can practically feel the glee emanating from this tweet so it's hilarious that people will still refuse to admit he's a full-blown communist," civil rights attorney David Pivtorak posted on X.

In April, New York City Mayor Mamdani and Hochul unveiled the joint legislative proposal targeting luxury second homes worth $5 million or more. Known as a pied-à-terre tax, the annual surcharge is expected to generate an estimated $500 million from affluent out-of-state property owners.

Mamdani has previously said revenue from the tax would go toward initiatives such as free childcare, cleaner streets and safer neighborhoods.

Critics, including billionaire Ken Griffin whose luxury New York City home was featured in Mamdani's controversial video announcing the tax, argue the tax will only drive more wealthy New Yorkers out of the state and bring jobs and investments with them.

Fox News Digital reached out to Mamdani's office for comment.

Fox Business's Kristen Altus contributed to this report

item.title: Viral video shows massive bison popping tourist vehicle's tire in Yellowstone National Park
item.description: Viral video captures a massive bison popping a tourist vehicle's tire in Yellowstone National Park, and the hilarious internet reactions are priceless.
item.content:encoded:
An incredible viral video shows a massive bison jacking up a vehicle.

There are few things more entertaining on the internet than great nature videos. I'm not even sure I can explain why that's the case.

It just is.

WOMAN RISKS BEING GORED BY BISON FOR A SELFIE AT BADLANDS NATIONAL PARK IN SHOCKING FOOTAGE

Whether it's something beautiful or something downright terrible, almost all of it is must-watch content, and that leads us to a video currently making the rounds.

TikTok user @tonya_names recently posted an awesome video showing a huge bison popping the tire on a tourist's vehicle.

"I think this bison knew we were NDSU Bison fans so picked on the car in front of us 😳 glad we escaped the bison hit," she wrote.

WOMAN ATTACKED BY BROWN BEAR WHILE HIKING WITH FRIEND AND 3 DOGS NEAR ALASKA STATE PARK

The incident took place in Yellowstone National Park.
